food_subgroup: Cantonese wonton soup — clear chicken-prawn broth with 6 pork-prawn wontons, 1 bowl (~400g total with broth)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Wonton Soup as a prepared composite dish. Closest parent would be a filled-dumpling soup concept; not yet represented in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Filled dumpling soup, wheat skin, chicken-pork broth (closest parent match)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0GZD used as closest parent for Cantonese wonton soup; no exact code exists for this specific preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Cantonese wonton soup as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of Cantonese wonton soup contains 6 wontons (~100g wonton solids) in approximately 300g of clear chicken-prawn broth, totalling ~400g.

nutrient_source.title: Wonton Soup — Component Calculation derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for pork, shrimp, wheat wonton wrapper, and chicken broth
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by component calculation using USDA SR Legacy entries for ground pork (FDC 168907), cooked shrimp, wheat-based wonton wrappers, and clear chicken broth. Values represent the composite dish at ~400g per bowl (100g wonton solids + 300g broth). Per-100g values reflect the full bowl composition including broth. Singapore HPB database does not carry a direct wonton soup entry; USDA SR Legacy component calculation is the highest-quality approved source available.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of wheat-based dumpling wrappers and analogous wheat noodle/pasta products — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Cantonese wonton soup in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of ~55 is estimated from the closest analogous foods: wheat-based dumpling/pasta wrappers (GI ~46–65 range in University of Sydney database) and the moderating effect of protein (pork + shrimp) and fat content on glucose absorption. The clear broth contributes negligible carbohydrate and essentially zero GL. Available carbohydrate per 400g bowl ~24g; GL = (55 × 24) / 100 = 13.2. This is a defensible analogous estimate consistent with published GI values for wheat-skin dumplings.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium — approximately 1,000mg per 400g bowl (250mg/100g), predominantly from the clear broth (~500mg invisible sodium in ~300g broth)
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should avoid finishing the broth — eating the wontons only and discarding the broth reduces sodium intake by approximately 300–350mg per bowl; if broth is consumed, limit to half a bowl and avoid adding soy sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~55 (low-moderate), GL ~13 per bowl; protein from pork and shrimp (~16g per bowl) moderates postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Wonton soup is one of the more T2DM-compatible Chinese soup dishes at GI ~55 and GL ~13 per bowl; patients should eat the wontons with broth in moderation and pair with non-starchy vegetables to further blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (~1,000mg/bowl) is the primary CVD concern; saturated fat is modest at ~2.8g per bowl; cholesterol ~44mg per bowl is clinically insignificant for most patients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, the dominant risk is sodium from the broth — limit broth consumption to reduce sodium load; the low saturated fat content (~0.7g/100g) and lean protein profile are otherwise compatible with a heart-healthy diet when broth intake is controlled.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium (1,000mg/bowl) may exacerbate fluid retention in patients on corticosteroids; shrimp provides selenium (~16mcg/100g) supporting immune function
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients on corticosteroids should limit broth consumption to reduce sodium-driven fluid retention; the selenium from shrimp (~16mcg/100g) supports thyroid and immune function relevant to RA management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cantonese Wonton Soup is a classic southern Chinese soup dish consisting of 6 boiled wontons served in a clear chicken-prawn broth, typically garnished with spring onion. Each wonton is made from a thin wheat-flour wrapper filled with a mixture of minced pork, chopped prawns (shrimp), and spring onion, then folded and boiled. The broth is a light, savoury clear stock made from chicken bones and dried shrimp, giving it a delicate umami flavour. The dish is widely available across Singapore's hawker centres, food courts, and Cantonese restaurants. One standard bowl (~400g total: ~100g wontons + ~300g broth) provides approximately 250 kcal, 16g protein, 9g fat, and 24g carbohydrate. The GI is estimated at ~55 (low-moderate) with a glycaemic load of ~13 per bowl, making it one of the more diabetes-compatible Chinese soup dishes. CRITICAL CLINICAL NOTE: The clear broth contains approximately 500mg of invisible sodium — patients and clinicians must not assume 'clear broth equals low sodium.' Total sodium per bowl is approximately 1,000mg (42–50% of the daily recommended limit). The single most effective dietary intervention for hypertensive patients eating wonton soup is to eat the wontons and discard or limit the broth. Shrimp cholesterol (~44mg per bowl) is clinically insignificant for most individuals. Source: USDA SR Legacy component calculation. Confidence: medium.
common_user_queries: how much sodium in wonton soup
meal_context: breakfast or lunch at Chinese kopitiam

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from USDA SR Legacy component calculation for the composite dish (pork, shrimp, wheat wonton wrapper, chicken broth) rather than a direct measurement of the prepared dish in an approved primary regional database. Singapore HPB database does not carry a direct wonton soup entry. GI is an analogous estimate from wheat-based dumpling/pasta data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Values are internally consistent and plausible for the described preparation.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite dish preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component calculation as of 2024. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish including broth was not separately reported in the component calculation source. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy source data for the component ingredients; no hydrogenated fats used in traditional Cantonese wonton preparation. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace per USDA SR Legacy for pork, shrimp, and chicken broth components; traditional preparation contains no significant vitamin D source. 5. FoodEx2 code A0GZD is used as closest parent; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Cantonese wonton soup. 6. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on wheat-skin dumpling/pasta analogues from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Cantonese wonton soup was found. 7. Sodium per 400g bowl ~1,000mg — the broth is the dominant invisible sodium source (~500mg in ~300g broth). 8. This record represents a restaurant/hawker-centre bowl portion; home preparation sodium may vary significantly.
